title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.2 From Symmetry and Reciprocal Space to Diffraction
text:
5.2 From Symmetry and Reciprocal Space to Diffraction
In Lecture 4 we introduced the reciprocal lattice, reciprocal-lattice vectors \(\mathbf G\), plane spacings \(d_{hkl}\), and the first Brillouin zone. Today these objects stop being purely geometrical and become directly measurable.
The central idea is simple:

the periodicity of the lattice selects special momentum transfers;
the basis inside the primitive cell weights those allowed transfers;
the measured diffraction pattern is therefore a reciprocal-space image of the crystal.

This is why diffraction is the experimental counterpart of the reciprocal-space formalism introduced in the previous lecture.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.3 Elastic Diffraction from a Periodic Lattice
text:
5.3 Elastic Diffraction from a Periodic Lattice

5.3.1 Bragg’s Geometric Picture
Suppose radiation of wavelength \(\lambda\) is reflected from a family of parallel lattice planes of spacing \(d_{hkl}\). Constructive interference occurs when the path difference between rays reflected from neighboring planes is an integer multiple of \(\lambda\).






\[
2 d_{hkl}\sin\theta = m\lambda,
\qquad
m\in\mathbb Z.
\]



This is the Bragg condition.

Interpretation:

only certain scattering angles are allowed for a given wavelength;
smaller plane spacing means larger scattering angle;
higher-order peaks correspond to larger reciprocal vectors, or equivalently to larger integers \(m\).







Figure 5.1: Figure placeholder. Reflection geometry for diffraction from a family of lattice planes with spacing \(d_{hkl}\), showing the path difference that leads to the Bragg condition.





5.3.2 Laue’s Reciprocal-Space Picture
Now describe the same process atom by atom. Let the incident beam have wave vector \(\mathbf k\) and the scattered beam \(\mathbf k'\). For two equivalent atoms separated by a lattice vector \(\mathbf R_n\), the phase difference is






\[
\Delta \phi
=
(\mathbf k-\mathbf k')\cdot \mathbf R_n.
\]



Constructive interference must hold for every lattice vector \(\mathbf R_n\). Since reciprocal-lattice vectors satisfy \(\mathbf G\cdot \mathbf R_n = 2\pi \times \text{integer}\), the diffraction condition is






\[
\mathbf k-\mathbf k'=\mathbf G.
\]



This is the Laue condition.

So the lattice does not allow arbitrary momentum transfer. In elastic diffraction, the scattering vector must be a reciprocal-lattice vector.


5.3.3 Equivalent Elastic Form
Because elastic scattering does not change the wavelength,






\[
|\mathbf k| = |\mathbf k'| = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}.
\]



Squaring the Laue condition gives a useful reciprocal-space criterion:






\[
\mathbf k\cdot \mathbf G = \frac{1}{2} |\mathbf G|^2.
\]



This says that diffraction occurs when the tip of the incident wave vector lies on a Bragg plane in reciprocal space.



5.3.4 Why Bragg and Laue Are the Same Statement
From Lecture 4 we know that reciprocal-lattice vectors normal to the \((hkl)\) planes satisfy






\[
|\mathbf G_{hkl}| = \frac{2\pi}{d_{hkl}}
\]



for the primitive reciprocal vector normal to that plane family. More generally, order \(m\) corresponds to the reciprocal vector \(m\mathbf G_{hkl}\). Combining this with \(|\mathbf G| = 2|\mathbf k|\sin\theta\) yields the Bragg condition again:






\[
2 d_{hkl}\sin\theta = m\lambda.
\]



So Bragg’s construction in direct space and Laue’s construction in reciprocal space are mathematically equivalent.



5.3.5 Sanity Check
The Bravais lattice determines which momentum transfers are allowed:

if \(\mathbf Q \equiv \mathbf k-\mathbf k'\) is not a reciprocal-lattice vector, the scattered amplitudes cancel almost completely;
if \(\mathbf Q=\mathbf G\), a Bragg peak may appear.

At this stage we have located the allowed peaks, but we have not yet explained why some are strong, weak, or absent. That information comes from the basis.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.4 From Bravais Lattice to Basis: Structure Factor
text:
5.4 From Bravais Lattice to Basis: Structure Factor

5.4.1 Total Scattering Amplitude
Let the primitive cell contain \(p\) atoms at positions \(\mathbf r_j\), and let atom \(j\) scatter with amplitude \(f_j(\mathbf Q)\). Then the total elastic scattering amplitude from the crystal is






\[
A(\mathbf Q)
=
\sum_n \sum_{j=1}^{p}
f_j(\mathbf Q)
e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot(\mathbf R_n+\mathbf r_j)}.
\]



The sum naturally factorizes into a lattice part and a basis part:
\[
A(\mathbf Q)
=
\left(
\sum_n e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf R_n}
\right)
\left(
\sum_{j=1}^{p} f_j(\mathbf Q)e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf r_j}
\right).
\]
Define the structure factor of one primitive cell by
\[
F(\mathbf Q)
=
\sum_{j=1}^{p} f_j(\mathbf Q)e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf r_j}.
\]
Then






\[
A(\mathbf Q)
=
\left(
\sum_n e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf R_n}
\right)
F(\mathbf Q).
\]






5.4.2 What the Two Factors Mean
The factorization is physically decisive:

\(\sum_n e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf R_n}\) encodes the Bravais lattice and produces the Laue selection rule \(\mathbf Q=\mathbf G\);
\(F(\mathbf G)\) encodes the basis and determines the intensity of the allowed Bragg peak.

For an ideal large crystal, the lattice sum is nonzero only at reciprocal-lattice vectors, so the measured Bragg intensity scales as






\[
I(\mathbf G)\propto |F(\mathbf G)|^2.
\]




This is the single most important equation of the lecture: peak positions come from the lattice; peak intensities come from the basis.


5.4.3 Atomic Form Factor
For X-ray scattering, the single-atom amplitude is the Fourier transform of the electronic charge distribution of that atom. In course notation we write






\[
f_j(\mathbf Q)
\propto
\int \rho_{e,j}(\mathbf r),e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf r},d^3r.
\]



This is the atomic form factor.

Consequences:

X-rays and electrons primarily probe electron density.
Heavier atoms often scatter more strongly in X-ray diffraction.
The basis weights are not just geometrical phase factors; they also depend on atomic species.

For nonmagnetic neutron scattering, the role of \(f_j\) is played by the nuclear scattering length, which is much less directly tied to atomic number.



5.4.4 What If the Basis Is Monatomic?
If there is only one atom in the primitive cell at the origin, then
\[
F(\mathbf G)=f(\mathbf G).
\]
All allowed peaks are then weighted only by the single-atom form factor. No extra phase cancellations occur inside the cell.
This is the baseline case against which basis effects are measured.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.5 Reciprocal-Space Selection Rules and Systematic Extinctions
text:
5.5 Reciprocal-Space Selection Rules and Systematic Extinctions
A reciprocal-lattice vector may satisfy the Laue condition and still give zero intensity because the basis contributions cancel. These are systematic extinctions.

5.5.1 Worked Example: Face-Centered Cubic Lattice
Index the structure with the conventional cubic cell of edge length \(a\). The four equivalent atomic positions are
\[
(0,0,0),
\qquad
\left(\frac12,\frac12,0\right),
\qquad
\left(\frac12,0,\frac12\right),
\qquad
\left(0,\frac12,\frac12\right).
\]
For identical atoms, the structure factor is
\[
F_{\mathrm{fcc}}(hkl)
=
f
\left[
1
+
e^{-i\pi(h+k)}
+
e^{-i\pi(h+l)}
+
e^{-i\pi(k+l)}
\right].
\]

Now evaluate the parity cases:

if \(h,k,l\) are all even or all odd, every exponential equals \(+1\), so \[
F_{\mathrm{fcc}}(hkl)=4f;
\]
if even and odd indices are mixed, the terms cancel and \[
F_{\mathrm{fcc}}(hkl)=0.
\]

Thus the selection rule is:






For fcc diffraction, only reflections with Miller indices all even or all odd are allowed.



This is a genuine structural fingerprint.


5.5.2 Interpretation
It is tempting to say that certain reciprocal-lattice points are “missing.” The deeper statement is subtler:

if one works with the true primitive cell, the reciprocal lattice is the correct one from the start;
if one indexes with the conventional cubic cell, the extinction appears as cancellation inside a larger nonprimitive cell.

So systematic extinctions are not arbitrary missing data. They encode the translational arrangement of the basis.







Figure 5.2: Figure placeholder. Primitive and conventional cells for the face-centered cubic lattice together with the reciprocal-space construction showing which indexed reciprocal points are allowed and which are extinguished.





5.5.3 Optional Extension: Diamond Structure
Diamond may be viewed as an fcc Bravais lattice plus a two-atom basis at
\[
\mathbf r_1 = \mathbf 0,
\qquad
\mathbf r_2 = \frac14(\mathbf a_1+\mathbf a_2+\mathbf a_3).
\]
Then the extra basis factor is
\[
F_{\mathrm{diamond}}(hkl)
=
F_{\mathrm{fcc}}(hkl)
\left[
1+e^{-i\pi(h+k+l)/2}
\right].
\]

Hence:

mixed parity is already forbidden by the fcc rule;
among all-even reflections, those with \(h+k+l = 4m+2\) are extinguished;
among all-odd reflections, the intensity is reduced relative to the strongest all-even family.

This is how diffraction distinguishes diamond from monatomic fcc structures.






Figure 5.3: Figure placeholder. Reciprocal-space points of the diamond structure grouped by strong, weak, and extinguished reflections due to interference between the two atoms of the basis.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.6 What Experiments Actually Measure
text:
5.6 What Experiments Actually Measure

5.6.1 The Ewald Construction
The reciprocal-space condition becomes visually transparent in the Ewald construction. Draw the incident wave vector \(\mathbf k\) with its tip on one reciprocal-lattice point, then draw a sphere of radius \(|\mathbf k|\) centered at the tail of \(\mathbf k\).
If another reciprocal-lattice point lies on that sphere, then a diffracted beam exists in the direction \(\mathbf k'\) from the sphere center to that point. Equivalently,
\[
\mathbf k-\mathbf k'=\mathbf G,
\qquad
|\mathbf k|=|\mathbf k'|.
\]

So diffraction is experimentally observed when the Ewald sphere intersects the reciprocal lattice.






Figure 5.4: Figure placeholder. Ewald construction showing how intersections of the Ewald sphere with reciprocal-lattice points determine elastic diffraction directions.





5.6.2 The Detector Does Not See the Crystal Directly
A diffraction experiment measures intensity as a function of scattering direction, and sometimes as a function of wavelength or sample orientation. In the simplest elastic setting:

the location of a Bragg peak determines the reciprocal-lattice vector \(\mathbf G\) and therefore the lattice geometry;
the intensity of that peak determines \(|F(\mathbf G)|^2\) and therefore constrains the basis;
the absence of a peak may be as informative as its presence.




5.6.3 The Phase Problem
If the complex amplitudes \(F(\mathbf G)\) were known, the lattice-periodic density could in principle be reconstructed by inverse Fourier transformation. In practice, standard diffraction measures intensities, not complex phases. Therefore one obtains
\[
I(\mathbf G)\propto |F(\mathbf G)|^2,
\]
but not the phase of \(F(\mathbf G)\).
This is the phase problem of diffraction.

Interpretation:

diffraction data strongly constrain the structure;
they do not uniquely reveal it without additional assumptions or refinement methods;
“what experiments actually measure” is therefore more limited than “the full microscopic density.”



5.6.4 Experimental Geometries in a Nutshell
There are the following classical ways to satisfy the diffraction condition:

the Laue method, using many wavelengths at fixed incident direction;
the rotating-crystal method, using a single crystal whose orientation is varied;
the powder method, using many grain orientations at once, which turns Bragg conditions into rings.

For this lecture’s purposes, all three are practical ways of sampling the same reciprocal-space condition.







Figure 5.5: Figure placeholder. Schematic diffraction setup with source, collimation, monochromator, sample, and detector, emphasizing that the experiment records scattered intensity versus angle.









Figure 5.6: Figure placeholder. Powder-diffraction geometry and the resulting ring or peak pattern, illustrating how a polycrystalline sample encodes reciprocal-lattice spacings.





5.6.5 Which Probe Tells You What?
The diffraction condition depends only on wavelength, but the probe–matter interaction determines what structural information is emphasized.

X-rays and electrons couple primarily to electron density.
Neutrons couple to nuclei and, when relevant, to magnetic moments.
Thermal neutrons naturally have wavelengths of order ångströms and energies of order \(0.1\ \mathrm{eV}\), which makes them especially useful once energy-resolved scattering is considered.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.7 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Elastic Diffraction to Inelastic Scattering
text:
5.7 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Elastic Diffraction to Inelastic Scattering
Elastic diffraction is powerful, but it is fundamentally a probe of the static periodic structure. In elastic scattering,






\[
\Delta E = 0,
\qquad
\mathbf Q = \mathbf k-\mathbf k'=\mathbf G.
\]



So elastic Bragg peaks tell us about lattice periodicity and basis interference, but not directly about the excitation spectrum of the solid.
By contrast, in inelastic scattering one measures both momentum transfer and energy transfer:






\[
\mathbf Q = \mathbf k-\mathbf k',
\qquad
\hbar\omega = E-E'.
\]



Now the scattered probe can create or absorb an excitation of the crystal. For lattice vibrations, the measured momentum transfer is decomposed as






\[
\mathbf Q = \mathbf G + \mathbf q,
\]



where \(\mathbf q\) is the reduced wave vector in the first Brillouin zone. The corresponding energy transfer reveals the phonon frequency \(\omega_s(\mathbf q)\).
This is the route from reciprocal-space diffraction geometry to experimentally measured phonon dispersions: elastic diffraction identifies the reciprocal lattice, while inelastic scattering maps how excitations disperse around it.
Thermal neutrons are especially well suited to this task because their wavelengths match lattice spacings and their energies are comparable to typical solid-state excitation energies.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.8 Take-Home Messages
text:
5.8 Take-Home Messages






NoteTake-Home Messages




Bragg’s law and the Laue condition are two equivalent ways of expressing elastic diffraction in a crystal.
The Bravais lattice determines the allowed peak positions in reciprocal space.
The basis determines the relative intensities of those peaks through the structure factor.
Systematic extinctions are structural information, not experimental accidents.
Reciprocal space becomes experimentally visible through the Ewald construction.
Standard diffraction measures intensities and angles, but not the phase of the complex scattering amplitude.
Different probes emphasize different microscopic constituents of the solid.
Elastic diffraction reveals static structure, while inelastic scattering is needed to determine phonon dispersions.

title: 5  Diffraction and Structure Factor
section: 5.9 Problem Set
text:
5.9 Problem Set

Lattice Versus Basis. For a crystal with basis positions \(\mathbf r_j\) and atomic amplitudes \(f_j(\mathbf Q)\), derive the factorized scattering amplitude \[
A(\mathbf Q)
=
\left(\sum_n e^{-i\mathbf Q\cdot \mathbf R_n}\right)
F(\mathbf Q).
\] Explain clearly which factor determines peak positions and which factor determines peak intensities.
fcc Extinction Rule. Using the four conventional-cell positions of an fcc lattice, derive \[
F_{\mathrm{fcc}}(hkl)
=
f
\left[
1
+
e^{-i\pi(h+k)}
+
e^{-i\pi(h+l)}
+
e^{-i\pi(k+l)}
\right].
\] Show that reflections occur only when \(h,k,l\) are all even or all odd.
Diamond as an fcc Lattice Plus Basis. Starting from the fcc rule, add a second identical atom at \(\frac14(\mathbf a_1+\mathbf a_2+\mathbf a_3)\) and derive the extra factor for diamond. Which all-even reflections are extinguished?

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.2 Why Lattice Dynamics Matters for Thermal Physics
text:
6.2 Why Lattice Dynamics Matters for Thermal Physics
A rigid lattice is not enough to understand the thermal behavior of a solid. At finite temperature, atoms fluctuate about their equilibrium positions, and these collective motions are essential for specific heat, thermal expansion, sound propagation, and thermal conductivity, especially in insulators and semiconductors. In this lecture we build the general harmonic formalism that makes those phenomena calculable.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.3 Born–Oppenheimer Approximation and Effective Potential for the Nuclei
text:
6.3 Born–Oppenheimer Approximation and Effective Potential for the Nuclei
The first simplification is not yet specific to crystals. A solid is a coupled quantum system of electrons and nuclei, so in principle one should solve the full many-body Schrödinger equation for both at once. The reason this is usually not attempted directly in lattice dynamics is the large mass difference between electrons and nuclei: the electronic degrees of freedom adjust much faster than the nuclear ones. This scale separation motivates the Born–Oppenheimer, or adiabatic, approximation.


6.3.1 Full electron–nucleus Hamiltonian
We begin from the non-relativistic Coulomb Hamiltonian for \(N_e\) electrons and \(N_n\) nuclei:






\[
H = T_e + T_n + V_{ee} + V_{nn} + V_{en},
\]



with \[
\begin{aligned}
T_e &= -\sum_{j=1}^{N_e}\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e}\nabla_{\mathbf r_j}^2,\\
T_n &= -\sum_{I=1}^{N_n}\frac{\hbar^2}{2M_I}\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}^2,\\
V_{ee} &= \sum_{i<j}\frac{e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0|\mathbf r_i-\mathbf r_j|},\\
V_{nn} &= \sum_{I<J}\frac{Z_I Z_J e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0|\mathbf R_I-\mathbf R_J|},\\
V_{en} &= -\sum_{j,I}\frac{Z_I e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0|\mathbf r_j-\mathbf R_I|},\\
\end{aligned}
\] where \(T_e\) represents the kinetic energy of the electrons, \(T_n\) the kinetic energy of the nuclei, \(V_{ee}\) the interaction among the electrons, \(V_{nn}\) the interaction among the nuclei, and \(V_{en}\) the interaction among electrons and nuclei.
The exact stationary problem is therefore \[
H\Psi({\mathbf r_j},{\mathbf R_I})=E\Psi({\mathbf r_j},{\mathbf R_I}).
\]


6.3.2 Electronic problem at fixed nuclei
Now freeze the nuclei at some instantaneous configuration \({\mathbf R_I}\) and group all remaining terms into an electronic Hamiltonian \[
H_e({\mathbf r_j};{\mathbf R_I})
=
T_e+V_{ee}+V_{en}+V_{nn}.
\]
For each fixed nuclear configuration one solves the electronic eigenvalue problem






\[
H_e({\mathbf r_j};{\mathbf R_I})
\psi_m({\mathbf r_j};{\mathbf R_I})
=
E_m({\mathbf R_I})
\psi_m({\mathbf r_j};{\mathbf R_I}).
\]



The eigenvalues \(E_m({\mathbf R_I})\) are electronic energy surfaces as functions of the nuclear coordinates. In lattice dynamics, the most important one is usually the ground-state surface \(E_0({\mathbf R_I})\).


6.3.3 Expansion of the full wavefunction
The exact total wavefunction may be expanded in the electronic basis: \[
\Psi({\mathbf r_j},{\mathbf R_I})
=
\sum_m
\chi_m({\mathbf R_I})
\psi_m({\mathbf r_j};{\mathbf R_I}).
\]
Substituting this into the full Schrödinger equation and projecting onto an electronic state yields a set of coupled nuclear equations. The only nontrivial point is that the nuclear kinetic-energy operator acts both on the nuclear amplitudes \(\chi_m\) and on the \({\mathbf R_I}\)-dependence of the electronic states \(\psi_m\). This produces derivative couplings between different electronic surfaces. Writing \[
\mathbf d_{nm}^{(I)}
=
\left\langle
\psi_n
\middle|
\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}\psi_m
\right\rangle_{\mathbf r},
\qquad
\tau_{nm}^{(I)}
=
\left\langle
\psi_n
\middle|
\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}^2\psi_m
\right\rangle_{\mathbf r},
\] one obtains the exact coupled equations \[
\sum_m
\left\{
-\sum_I \frac{\hbar^2}{2M_I}
\left[
\delta_{nm}\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}^2
+2\mathbf d_{nm}^{(I)}\cdot\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}
+\tau_{nm}^{(I)}
\right]
+\delta_{nm}E_m({\mathbf R_I})
\right\}
\chi_m
=
E\chi_n.
\]


6.3.4 Born–Oppenheimer approximation
The Born–Oppenheimer approximation assumes that nuclear motion is slow enough that transitions between different electronic surfaces are negligible. Mathematically, one neglects the non-adiabatic couplings and keeps only one electronic surface, typically the ground state \(m=0\). Then the nuclear problem reduces to






\[
\left[
-\sum_I\frac{\hbar^2}{2M_I}\nabla_{\mathbf R_I}^2
+
E_0({\mathbf R_I})
\right]
\chi_0({\mathbf R_I})
=
E\chi_0({\mathbf R_I}).
\]



This is the precise meaning of the statement that the nuclei move in an effective potential: the potential energy surface is the electronic ground-state energy as a function of all nuclear coordinates.



6.3.5 Transition to the Harmonic Approximation
The Born–Oppenheimer approximation has now reduced the problem to nuclear motion on a multidimensional potential-energy surface. For lattice dynamics, the next step is to specialize to a periodic equilibrium crystal structure and assume that the nuclei perform only small oscillations about it. Then \(E_0({\mathbf R_I})\) can be expanded in powers of the nuclear displacements. Keeping only terms up to second order gives the harmonic approximation, which is the central working model of this lecture.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.4 Geometry and Indexing of a Crystal with a Basis
text:
6.4 Geometry and Indexing of a Crystal with a Basis
For a crystal with \(p\) atoms in the primitive cell, we label cells by Bravais-lattice vectors \(\mathbf R_m\) and basis atoms by \(\mu=1,\dots,p\). The equilibrium position of atom \(\mu\) in cell \(m\) is






\[
\mathbf r^0(m,\mu)=\mathbf R_m+\mathbf r_\mu,
\]



and its instantaneous position is






\[
\mathbf r(m,\mu,t)=\mathbf R_m+\mathbf r_\mu+\mathbf u(m,\mu,t).
\]



The displacement field \(\mathbf u(m,\mu,t)\) is the central dynamical variable of the theory.







Figure 6.1: Figure placeholder. Indexing of primitive cells and basis atoms, showing the Bravais-lattice vector, the basis position inside the cell, and the displacement from equilibrium.




6.4.1 Sanity Check
The split \[
\text{position}=\text{cell origin}+\text{basis position}+\text{displacement}
\] cleanly separates crystal geometry from dynamics. The periodic structure is represented by \(\mathbf R_m\) and \(\mathbf r_\mu\), while all time dependence is in \(\mathbf u(m,\mu,t)\).

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.5 Harmonic Approximation and Force Constants
text:
6.5 Harmonic Approximation and Force Constants
Within the Born–Oppenheimer approximation, the effective potential is the electronic ground-state energy evaluated at the instantaneous nuclear coordinates. We therefore expand that effective potential in powers of the small displacements around equilibrium:






\[
U = U_0
+ \sum_{m,\mu,\alpha} \Phi^\mu_\alpha(m) u_\alpha(m,\mu)
+ \frac12 \sum_{m,\mu,\alpha}\sum_{n,\nu,\beta}
  \Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)
  u_\alpha(m,\mu) u_\beta(n,\nu)
+ \cdots
\]
with \[
\Phi^\mu_\alpha(m)=
\frac{\partial U}{\partial u_\alpha(m,\mu)},
\qquad
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)=
\frac{\partial^2 U}
{\partial u_\alpha(m,\mu),\partial u_\beta(n,\nu)}.
\]



At equilibrium the linear term vanishes, because the first derivatives of the potential are zero there. Keeping only the quadratic term defines the harmonic approximation. The coefficients \(\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)\) are the force constants: they are the many-body generalization of spring constants.


6.5.1 Symmetry and Translational Invariance
Because mixed partial derivatives commute, \[
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)
=
\Phi_{\beta\alpha}^{\nu\mu}(n,m).
\]
Because a rigid translation of the entire crystal cannot generate restoring forces, the force constants satisfy the sum rule \[
\sum_{n,\nu}\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)=0.
\]
Finally, in an ordered crystal translational symmetry implies \[
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)
=
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m-n),
\] so the couplings depend only on the difference between cell labels, not on absolute position.

A particularly useful equivalent form of the harmonic potential is \[
U_{\mathrm{harm}}
=
-\frac14
\sum_{m,\mu,\alpha}\sum_{n,\nu,\beta}
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)
\big[u_\alpha(m,\mu)-u_\alpha(n,\nu)\big]
\big[u_\beta(m,\mu)-u_\beta(n,\nu)\big].
\]


6.5.2 Interpretation
This form makes the physics transparent: only relative displacements matter. A rigid translation leaves all displacement differences unchanged and therefore costs no energy.


6.5.3 Brief Remark on Microscopic Meaning
For central pair forces, the restoring force depends strongly on the component of the relative displacement along the line joining the atoms. This is why bond geometry enters lattice dynamics so directly, even within a harmonic description.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.6 Equations of Motion in Real Space
text:
6.6 Equations of Motion in Real Space
The kinetic energy of the vibrating lattice is






\[
T_{\mathrm{kin}}
=
\sum_{m,\mu}
\frac12 M_\mu \dot{\mathbf u}^2(m,\mu,t),
\]



and the harmonic equations of motion are






\[
M_\mu \ddot u_\alpha(m,\mu,t)
=
-
\sum_{n,\nu,\beta}
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m-n)
u_\beta(n,\nu,t).
\]



Thus the crystal is a system of \(3pN\) coupled linear oscillators if there are \(N\) primitive cells and \(p\) atoms per cell.


6.6.1 Sanity Check
The equations are linear because we stopped at quadratic order in the potential. That linearity is exactly what makes a normal-mode decomposition possible.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.7 Plane-Wave Ansatz and the Dynamical Matrix
text:
6.7 Plane-Wave Ansatz and the Dynamical Matrix
A periodic crystal strongly suggests a Fourier description. Under Born–von Kármán boundary conditions, the allowed wavevectors are discrete and can be chosen inside the first Brillouin zone. In three dimensions one may write \[
\mathbf q
=
\frac{n_1}{N_1}\mathbf b_1
+
\frac{n_2}{N_2}\mathbf b_2
+
\frac{n_3}{N_3}\mathbf b_3,
\] with reciprocal-lattice vectors \(\mathbf b_i\). Wavevectors that differ by a reciprocal-lattice vector describe the same displacement pattern, so the first Brillouin zone contains the physically distinct choices.

For a single normal mode we use a mass-weighted plane-wave ansatz, \[
u_\alpha(m,\mu,t)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{M_\mu}}
e_{\mu,\alpha}^{(\lambda)}(\mathbf q)
e^{i(\mathbf q\cdot\mathbf R_m-\omega t)}.
\]
Substituting this into the real-space equations of motion yields the eigenvalue problem






\[
\sum_{\nu,\beta}
D_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(\mathbf q)
e_{\nu,\beta}^{(\lambda)}(\mathbf q)
=
\omega_\lambda^2(\mathbf q)
e_{\mu,\alpha}^{(\lambda)}(\mathbf q),
\] with \[
D_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(\mathbf q)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{M_\mu M_\nu}}
\sum_{\ell}
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(\ell)
e^{-i\mathbf q\cdot\mathbf R_\ell}.
\]



The matrix \(D(\mathbf q)\) is the dynamical matrix. Its eigenvalues are the squared normal-mode frequencies, and its eigenvectors give the polarization pattern inside the primitive cell.


6.7.1 Why This Is the Key Simplification
Translational symmetry decouples different wavevectors. For each fixed \(\mathbf q\), only the \(3p\) displacement amplitudes inside the primitive cell remain coupled. The original \(3pN\)-dimensional problem has therefore been reduced to diagonalizing a \(3p\times 3p\) matrix for each \(\mathbf q\).


6.7.2 Hermiticity and Positivity
The dynamical matrix is Hermitian, \[
D_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(\mathbf q)
=
\big[D_{\beta\alpha}^{\nu\mu}(\mathbf q)\big]^{*},
\] so its eigenvalues are real. Because the harmonic expansion is taken around an energy minimum, the eigenvalues are also nonnegative. Therefore \(\omega_\lambda^2(\mathbf q)\ge 0\).

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.8 Branch Counting and Mode Classification
text:
6.8 Branch Counting and Mode Classification
For each \(\mathbf q\), the dynamical matrix has dimension \(3p\times 3p\). Therefore a crystal with \(p\) atoms in the primitive cell has
\[
3p
\]
vibrational branches.

6.8.1 Acoustic Branches
At \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\), a rigid translation of the entire crystal must cost no energy. In the mass-weighted eigenproblem this corresponds to eigenvectors of the form \[
\mathbf e_\mu^{(\lambda)}(\mathbf 0)
\propto
\sqrt{M_\mu} \hat{\mathbf e}^{(\lambda)},
\] where \(\hat{\mathbf e}^{(\lambda)}\) is a unit vector in one of three mutually perpendicular directions. Hence there are always three zero-frequency solutions at \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\), and therefore always three acoustic branches.

For small \(|\mathbf q|\), all atoms in a primitive cell move almost in phase and with nearly equal amplitudes. These modes become the elastic sound waves of continuum theory in the long-wavelength limit.


6.8.2 Optical Branches
The remaining \[
3p-3
\] branches are optical branches. They have finite frequency at \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\) in general and correspond to internal motion of the basis rather than rigid translation of the crystal.
For a diatomic basis, the long-wavelength optical motion satisfies \[
M_1 \mathbf u_1 + M_2 \mathbf u_2 = 0.
\] So the center of mass of the primitive cell remains fixed while the two sublattices move against each other.

If the two sublattices carry opposite effective charges, such a mode can produce a dipole moment and couple to light. This explains the name optical. However, the label is kinematic, not spectroscopic: “optical” means “finite frequency at \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\),” not automatically “optically active”. In diamond-type crystals, for example, optical modes exist although the \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\) optical mode does not generate a dipole moment.



6.8.3 Longitudinal and Transverse Modes
In directions of high symmetry, one may classify modes by the orientation of the polarization vector relative to \(\mathbf q\):

longitudinal: polarization parallel to \(\mathbf q\),
transverse: polarization perpendicular to \(\mathbf q\).

For a general propagation direction, however, the eigenvectors need not be purely parallel or perpendicular to \(\mathbf q\); mixed character is possible.







Figure 6.2: Figure placeholder. Example phonon branches of crystalline silicon along high-symmetry directions, labeled TA, LA, TO, and LO. This figure is useful here only as a classification map; the explicit construction of such dispersions is deferred to the next lecture.





6.8.4 Sanity Checks

If \(p=1\), then \(3p-3=0\): a monatomic crystal has no optical branches.
If \(p>1\), optical branches are possible because the basis has internal degrees of freedom.
The existence of three acoustic branches is tied directly to the three independent rigid translations of a three-dimensional crystal.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.9 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Formalism to Explicit Dispersions
text:
6.9 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Formalism to Explicit Dispersions
Today we built the general framework. We now know what the variables are, how the Born–Oppenheimer approximation produces an effective potential for the nuclei, what the harmonic approximation means, how the equations of motion arise, and why the problem reduces to a dynamical-matrix eigenvalue equation.
In the next lecture we will make this framework concrete. We will choose explicit one-dimensional models (first with one atom per cell, then with two atoms per cell), construct \(D(\mathbf q)\) explicitly, and extract:

dispersion relations \(\omega_\lambda(\mathbf q)\),
the meaning of zone-center and zone-boundary motion,
acoustic versus optical behavior in a fully worked example,
and the bridge from dispersion to the density of states.

That is where group velocity, band folding, and the first direct connection to heat-capacity calculations will enter.






NoteTake-Home Messages




The Born–Oppenheimer approximation turns the full electron–nucleus problem into nuclear motion in an effective potential.
In lattice dynamics, that effective potential is expanded about the equilibrium crystal structure.
The harmonic approximation keeps only quadratic terms in the displacements and leads to linear equations of motion.
Force constants are generalized spring constants, constrained by symmetry and by rigid-translation sum rules.
Translational symmetry is what makes the plane-wave reduction possible.
For each wavevector, the vibrational problem is the diagonalization of a \(3p\times 3p\) dynamical matrix.
Every three-dimensional crystal has three acoustic branches.
Optical branches exist only when the primitive cell contains internal degrees of freedom beyond rigid translation.

title: 6  Lattice Dynamics I
section: 6.10 Problem Set
text:
6.10 Problem Set

Why does the linear term vanish? In the Taylor expansion of the effective potential about equilibrium, explain why the term linear in the displacements must vanish. Then show why \[
\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)
=
\Phi_{\beta\alpha}^{\nu\mu}(n,m)
\] follows from the definition of the force constants.
Translational-invariance sum rule. Consider a uniform displacement of the whole crystal, \[
\mathbf u(m,\mu)=\mathbf u_0
\qquad \text{for all } m,\mu.
\] Starting from the harmonic force law, show that the force on every atom vanishes only if \[
\sum_{n,\nu}\Phi_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(m,n)=0.
\] What is the physical content of this result?
Deriving the dynamical matrix. Insert the mass-weighted plane-wave ansatz \[
u_\alpha(m,\mu,t)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{M_\mu}}
e_{\mu,\alpha}(\mathbf q)
e^{i(\mathbf q\cdot\mathbf R_m-\omega t)}
\] into the real-space equations of motion and derive the eigenvalue problem \[
\sum_{\nu,\beta}
D_{\alpha\beta}^{\mu\nu}(\mathbf q)
e_{\nu,\beta}(\mathbf q)
=
\omega^2
e_{\mu,\alpha}(\mathbf q).
\] Why do different wavevectors decouple?
Branch counting. For crystals with \(p=1\), \(p=2\), and \(p=4\) atoms in the primitive cell, count the total number of branches, the number of acoustic branches, and the number of optical branches. Then state whether diamond-structure silicon has optical branches and whether its \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\) optical mode must be infrared active.
Meaning of a long-wavelength optical mode. In a crystal with a diatomic basis, explain the physical meaning of \[
M_1\mathbf u_1+M_2\mathbf u_2=0
\] for a \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\) optical mode. What does this imply if \(M_1=M_2\)?

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
text: 7.1 Roadmap

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
text:
Apply the general harmonic formalism of Lecture 6 in explicit one-dimensional models.
Monatomic chain: equations of motion, allowed \(q\)-values, dispersion, and group velocity.
Diatomic chain: coupled equations, acoustic/optical branches, and mode character.
Bridge: \(q\)-counting \(\rightarrow\) continuum limit \(\rightarrow\) dispersion \(\rightarrow\) density of states.
Optional appendix: equal-mass limit and band folding.

Meaning

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.2 From General Formalism to Explicit Chains
text:
7.2 From General Formalism to Explicit Chains
In Lecture 6 we reduced harmonic lattice dynamics to an eigenvalue problem for the dynamical matrix. Based on this, we work through the simplest explicit models analytically. The reward is that several abstract concepts from the previous lecture become concrete:

Brillouin-zone restriction,
dispersion relations,
group velocity,
acoustic versus optical branches,
and the counting argument that leads directly to a density of states.

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.3 Monatomic Linear Chain
text:
7.3 Monatomic Linear Chain
We begin with a one-dimensional chain of identical atoms of mass \(M\), equally spaced by the lattice constant \(a\). Only longitudinal displacements are kept, and only nearest-neighbor couplings are retained.







Figure 7.1: Figure placeholder. Geometry of the monatomic linear chain: equilibrium positions, longitudinal displacements, and nearest-neighbor elastic couplings.




7.3.1 Model and Harmonic Energy
Let the equilibrium position of atom \(n\) be \[
x_n^{(0)} = n a,
\] and let \(u_n(t)\) denote its displacement from equilibrium. The harmonic potential energy is \[
U = \frac{K}{2}\sum_n \left(u_{n+1}-u_n\right)^2.
\]
This is the one-dimensional specialization of the harmonic lattice energy from Lecture 6: only relative displacements matter, and only nearest neighbors are coupled.



7.3.2 Equation of Motion
Differentiating the potential with respect to \(u_n\) gives \[
M\ddot u_n = -K\left(2u_n-u_{n-1}-u_{n+1}\right).
\]
This is the discrete wave equation for the monatomic chain.






\[
M\ddot u_n + K\left(2u_n-u_{n-1}-u_{n+1}\right)=0.
\]






7.3.3 Born–von Kármán Boundary Conditions and Allowed \(q\)-Values
To keep the algebra simple, we impose periodic boundary conditions: \[
u_{n+N}(t)=u_n(t).
\]
We then seek plane-wave solutions of the form \[
u_n(t)=u(q)e^{i(qna-\omega t)}.
\]
The boundary condition gives \[
e^{iqNa}=1,
\] hence \[
q = \frac{2\pi j}{Na},
\qquad
j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
However, \(q\) and \(q+G\) with reciprocal-lattice vector \[
G=\frac{2\pi}{a}
\] describe the same displacement pattern on the discrete lattice sites, since \[
e^{i(q+G)na}=e^{iqna}e^{i2\pi n}=e^{iqna}.
\]
Therefore only \(N\) distinct wavevectors exist, and they may be chosen inside the first Brillouin zone: \[
-\frac{\pi}{a}<q\le \frac{\pi}{a}.
\]






\[
q_j=\frac{2\pi j}{L},
\qquad
L=Na,
\qquad
-\frac{\pi}{a}<q\le \frac{\pi}{a}.
\]










Figure 7.2: Figure placeholder. Equivalence of the Fourier components with wavevectors \(q\) and \(q+G\) on a discrete lattice, illustrating why the vibrational spectrum can be restricted to the first Brillouin zone.





7.3.4 Dispersion Relation
Substituting the plane-wave ansatz into the equation of motion yields \[
-M\omega^2 u(q)
=
-K\left(2-e^{-iqa}-e^{iqa}\right)u(q)
=
-2K\left(1-\cos qa\right)u(q).
\]
For nonzero amplitude, \[
\omega^2(q)=\frac{2K}{M}\left(1-\cos qa\right).
\]
Equivalently,






\[
\omega(q)=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\left|\sin\frac{qa}{2}\right|.
\]



This is the dispersion relation of the monatomic linear chain.



7.3.5 Long-Wavelength Limit and Sound Speed
For \(|qa|\ll 1\), \[
\sin\frac{qa}{2}\approx \frac{qa}{2},
\] so the dispersion becomes linear: \[
\omega(q)\approx a\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}} |q|.
\]
Thus the chain behaves as an elastic continuum in the long-wavelength limit. The slope at the zone center is the sound speed, \[
v_s = a\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}.
\]
This is why the branch is called acoustic.



7.3.6 Group Velocity
The group velocity of a wave packet is \[
v_g(q)=\frac{d\omega}{dq}.
\]
For \(0\le q\le \pi/a\), where the absolute value may be dropped, \[
\omega(q)=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\sin\frac{qa}{2},
\] so \[
v_g(q)=a\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\cos\frac{qa}{2}.
\]
Hence \[
v_g(0)=v_s,
\qquad
v_g \left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)=0.
\]
The vanishing of \(v_g\) at the zone boundary means that the dispersion flattens there: zone-edge modes do not transport energy through the chain as a wave packet.






\[
v_g(q)=\frac{d\omega}{dq}
=
a\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\cos\frac{qa}{2}
\qquad
(0\le q\le \pi/a).
\]










Figure 7.3: Figure placeholder. Dispersion relation \(\omega(q)\) of the monatomic linear chain, showing linear acoustic behavior near \(q=0\) and zero group velocity at the Brillouin-zone edge.





7.3.7 Interpretation

\(\omega(q)=\omega(-q)\), as expected from left-right symmetry and time-reversal invariance.
\(\omega(0)=0\): a rigid translation of the chain costs no energy.
The maximum frequency occurs at \(|q|=\pi/a\): \[
\omega_{\max}=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}.
\]
In the ideal 1D chain, only longitudinal modes are supported in the harmonic approximation; transverse restoring forces are absent in this model.

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.4 Diatomic Linear Chain
text:
7.4 Diatomic Linear Chain
We now place two atoms in each primitive cell: one of mass \(M_1\) at position \(na\), and one of mass \(M_2\) at position \(na+a/2\). Let their displacements in cell \(n\) be \(u_n(t)\) and \(v_n(t)\), respectively. As before, only nearest-neighbor couplings and longitudinal motion are retained.







Figure 7.4: Figure placeholder. Primitive cell and displacements of the diatomic linear chain, with masses \(M_1\) and \(M_2\) alternating along the chain.




7.4.1 Harmonic Energy and Equations of Motion
The harmonic potential energy is \[
U
=
\frac{K}{2}\sum_n (u_n-v_n)^2
+
\frac{K}{2}\sum_n (v_n-u_{n+1})^2.
\]
The equations of motion are therefore \[
M_1\ddot u_n = -K(2u_n-v_n-v_{n-1}),
\] \[
M_2\ddot v_n = -K(2v_n-u_n-u_{n+1}).
\]



7.4.2 Plane-Wave Ansatz and \(2\times 2\) Eigenvalue Problem
We use \[
u_n(t)=u(q)e^{i(qna-\omega t)},
\qquad
v_n(t)=v(q)e^{i(qna-\omega t)}.
\]
This gives \[
-\omega^2 M_1 u = -2Ku + K(1+e^{-iqa})v,
\] \[
-\omega^2 M_2 v = -2Kv + K(1+e^{iqa})u.
\]
Equivalently, \[
\begin{pmatrix}
2K-M_1\omega^2 & -K(1+e^{-iqa}) \\
-K(1+e^{iqa}) & 2K-M_2\omega^2
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\
v
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
0\\
0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Nontrivial solutions exist only if the determinant vanishes.



7.4.3 Acoustic and Optical Branches
Setting the determinant to zero yields two branches:






\[
\omega_\pm^2(q)
=
\frac{K}{M_1M_2}
\left[
(M_1+M_2)
\pm
\sqrt{(M_1+M_2)^2-4M_1M_2\sin^2\frac{qa}{2}}
\right].
\]



There are now two frequencies for each allowed \(q\), because the primitive cell contains two dynamical degrees of freedom along the chain.



7.4.4 Zone Center: In-Phase and Out-of-Phase Motion
At \(q=0\), the off-diagonal coupling is maximal because \(1+e^{iqa}=2\). The amplitude ratio becomes \[
\frac{v(0)}{u(0)}
=
\begin{cases}
+1, & \text{acoustic branch } \omega_-(q),\\
-\dfrac{M_1}{M_2}, & \text{optical branch } \omega_+(q).
\end{cases}
\]
So:

in the acoustic branch, the two atoms move in phase and the whole primitive cell is translated;
in the optical branch, the two atoms move against each other and the center of mass remains fixed.

Indeed, for the optical mode at \(q=0\), \[
M_1 u + M_2 v = 0.
\]
The zone-center optical frequency is \[
\omega_+(0)=\sqrt{2K\left(\frac{1}{M_1}+\frac{1}{M_2}\right)}.
\]



7.4.5 Long-Wavelength Acoustic Limit
Expanding the lower branch for \(|qa|\ll 1\) gives \[
\omega_-(q)\approx a|q|\sqrt{\frac{K}{2(M_1+M_2)}}.
\]
Thus the acoustic branch is again linear near \(q=0\), but the effective inertia is now set by the total mass in the primitive cell.



7.4.6 Zone Boundary: Sublattice-Selective Motion
At the Brillouin-zone boundary, \(q=\pi/a\), one has \[
1+e^{iqa}=1+e^{i\pi}=0,
\] so the two amplitudes decouple. The allowed frequencies are therefore \[
\omega^2=\frac{2K}{M_1}
\qquad\text{or}\qquad
\omega^2=\frac{2K}{M_2}.
\]
If \(M_1>M_2\), then

the lower-frequency branch is \[
\omega_-\left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2K}{M_1}},
\] and only the heavier sublattice moves;
the higher-frequency branch is \[
\omega_+\left(\frac{\pi}{a}\right)=\sqrt{\frac{2K}{M_2}},
\] and only the lighter sublattice moves.

This is a very useful physical picture: at the zone edge, the normal modes can become localized on one sublattice.



7.4.7 Why the Terms “Acoustic” and “Optical” Make Sense
The lower branch is called acoustic because it behaves like a sound wave in the long-wavelength limit: neighboring cells oscillate almost in phase and \(\omega_-(q)\to 0\) as \(q\to 0\).
The upper branch is called optical because its frequency remains finite at \(q=0\) and, in ionic chains, the relative motion of oppositely charged sublattices produces a dipole moment that can couple to light.







Figure 7.5: Figure placeholder. Acoustic and optical branches of the diatomic linear chain, showing the linear acoustic branch near \(q=0\) and the finite optical frequency at the zone center.









Figure 7.6: Figure placeholder. Mode character of the diatomic chain at the Brillouin-zone center and boundary: in-phase acoustic motion, out-of-phase optical motion, and the sublattice-selective zone-edge modes.





7.4.8 Interpretation

Two atoms per primitive cell \(\rightarrow\) two longitudinal branches in this 1D model.
The acoustic branch always lies below the optical branch for unequal masses.
In the long-wavelength limit, the acoustic mode translates each cell almost rigidly.
The optical mode describes internal motion of the basis.

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.5 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Allowed \(q\)-Values to a Density of States
text:
7.5 Bridge to the Next Lecture: From Allowed \(q\)-Values to a Density of States
The worked chains above do more than provide explicit dispersions. They also show how one passes from a discrete crystal to a continuum description in reciprocal space, which is the starting point for thermal calculations.


7.5.1 Step 1: Count the Allowed \(q\)-Values
For a chain of length \(L=Na\) with periodic boundary conditions, \[
q_j=\frac{2\pi j}{L}.
\]
Hence the spacing between neighboring wavevectors is \[
\Delta q = \frac{2\pi}{L}.
\]
So in the large-\(N\) limit, the number of states in an interval \(dq\) is \[
\frac{dq}{\Delta q}=\frac{L}{2\pi},dq.
\]
For each branch, this is the fundamental counting rule that converts sums over discrete \(q\) into integrals: \[
\sum_q f(q)
\longrightarrow
\frac{L}{2\pi}\int_{\mathrm{BZ}} f(q),dq.
\]
For an even dispersion, one often writes instead \[
\sum_q f(q)
\longrightarrow
\frac{L}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi/a} f(q),dq.
\]


7.5.2 Step 2: Convert Mode Counting from \(q\) to \(\omega\)
Suppose a branch \(\lambda\) has dispersion \(\omega_\lambda(q)\). Then the density of states of that branch is defined by \[
g_\lambda(\omega)=\sum_q \delta \left(\omega-\omega_\lambda(q)\right).
\]
In the continuum limit, \[
g_\lambda(\omega)
=
\frac{L}{2\pi}
\sum_{q_i(\omega)}
\left|
\frac{d\omega_\lambda}{dq}
\right|_{q_i}^{-1},
\] where the sum runs over all wavevectors \(q_i\) that satisfy \(\omega_\lambda(q_i)=\omega\).
Thus the density of states is controlled by the inverse slope of the dispersion. Equivalently, \[
g_\lambda(\omega)\propto \left|\frac{dq}{d\omega}\right|.
\]
This is the central logic: \[
\boxed{\text{mode counting in }q}
\rightarrow
\boxed{\omega(q)}
\rightarrow
\boxed{g(\omega)}.
\]


7.5.3 Step 3: Monatomic-Chain Example
For the monatomic chain, \[
\omega(q)=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\left|\sin\frac{qa}{2}\right|,
\qquad
\omega_{\max}=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}.
\]
For \(0<\omega<\omega_{\max}\) there are two solutions, \(\pm q\), with \[
q(\omega)=\frac{2}{a}\arcsin!\left(\frac{\omega}{\omega_{\max}}\right).
\]
Therefore \[
\left|\frac{dq}{d\omega}\right|
=
\frac{2}{a\omega_{\max}}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\omega/\omega_{\max})^2}},
\] and the density of states becomes






\[
g(\omega)
=
\frac{2L}{\pi a\omega_{\max}}
\frac{1}{\sqrt{1-(\omega/\omega_{\max})^2}}
\qquad
(0<\omega<\omega_{\max}).
\]



The divergence at \(\omega=\omega_{\max}\) occurs because \[
v_g=\frac{d\omega}{dq}\to 0
\] at the Brillouin-zone edge. In one dimension this is the simplest example of a van Hove singularity.


7.5.4 What Carries Over to More General Lattices?
The same logic survives in higher dimensions and with multiple branches:

count the allowed \(\mathbf q\) values,
determine the dispersion \(\omega_\lambda(\mathbf q)\),
convert the counting measure in \(\mathbf q\)-space into a density in frequency space,
sum over branches.

This is the handoff to the next stage of the course: once the normal modes are quantized and a density of states is known, thermal quantities such as internal energy and heat capacity can be computed systematically.

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.6 Optional / Appendix: Equal-Mass Limit and Band Folding
text:
7.6 Optional / Appendix: Equal-Mass Limit and Band Folding


Open optional appendix

If the two masses become equal, \(M_1=M_2=M\), the diatomic-chain result reduces to \[
\omega_\pm^2(q)=\frac{2K}{M}\left[1\pm \cos\frac{qa}{2}\right].
\]
Thus one may write \[
\omega_{\mathrm{ac}}(q)=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\left|\sin\frac{qa}{4}\right|,
\qquad
\omega_{\mathrm{op}}(q)=2\sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}\left|\cos\frac{qa}{4}\right|.
\]
The apparent “optical” branch is then not a genuinely new branch. The true primitive cell has length \(a/2\), so the true Brillouin-zone edge is at \(2\pi/a\), not \(\pi/a\). In the smaller Brillouin zone associated with period \(a\), the monatomic dispersion is simply folded back and appears as two branches.
This is a useful reminder that branch counting must always be done with the smallest primitive cell.








NoteTake-Home Messages




The monatomic linear chain has one longitudinal acoustic branch with a finite Brillouin-zone cutoff.
Restricting \(q\) to the first Brillouin zone is not a loss of information; wavevectors that differ by a reciprocal-lattice vector represent the same lattice displacement pattern.
The long-wavelength part of the monatomic dispersion is linear and defines the sound speed.
The group velocity is the slope of the dispersion and vanishes at the zone boundary.
A diatomic primitive cell produces two branches: one acoustic and one optical.
At the zone center, the acoustic mode translates the cell, whereas the optical mode describes internal motion of the basis.
At the zone boundary of the diatomic chain, the normal modes can become confined to one sublattice.
The density of states follows from counting allowed \(q\)-values and transforming that counting through the dispersion relation.

title: 7  Lattice Dynamics II
section: 7.7 Problem Set
text:
7.7 Problem Set

Monatomic chain: dispersion and sound speed. Starting from \[
U=\frac{K}{2}\sum_n (u_{n+1}-u_n)^2,
\] derive the equation of motion of the monatomic chain, insert the plane-wave ansatz \[
u_n(t)=u(q)e^{i(qna-\omega t)},
\] and obtain the dispersion relation. Then determine the sound speed from the long-wavelength limit.
Allowed wavevectors and Brillouin-zone restriction. For a monatomic chain of length \(L=Na\) with periodic boundary conditions, derive the allowed values of \(q\). Show explicitly that \(q\) and \(q+2\pi/a\) generate the same displacement pattern on the lattice sites, and explain why one may restrict \(q\) to the first Brillouin zone.
Diatomic chain: branch structure and mode character. Starting from \[
U
=
\frac{K}{2}\sum_n (u_n-v_n)^2
+
\frac{K}{2}\sum_n (v_n-u_{n+1})^2,
\] derive the coupled equations of motion and the two branches \(\omega_\pm(q)\). Then determine the amplitude ratio \(v/u\) at \(q=0\) and explain the physical meaning of each branch.
From dispersion to density of states. Use the allowed-\(q\) counting for the monatomic chain to derive the density of states \[
g(\omega)
=
\frac{L}{2\pi}
\sum_{q_i(\omega)}
\left|
\frac{d\omega}{dq}
\right|^{-1}_{q_i}.
\] Evaluate this explicitly for the monatomic-chain dispersion and identify the frequency at which the density of states diverges.

\(\mathbf q\)

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.2 From Harmonic Normal Modes to Phonons
text:
8.2 From Harmonic Normal Modes to Phonons
In the previous lecture we diagonalized the harmonic lattice problem and expressed the crystal motion in terms of independent normal modes. The essential next step is conceptual rather than structural: each of these decoupled harmonic modes is quantized in exactly the same way as a single quantum harmonic oscillator.


8.2.1 Harmonic Hamiltonian in Normal Coordinates
In normal coordinates, the harmonic lattice Hamiltonian is a sum of independent oscillators, \[
H_{\mathrm{harm}}
=
\frac{1}{2}
\sum_{\mathbf q,\lambda}
\left[
P_{\lambda}^{\dagger}(\mathbf q)P_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
+
\omega_{\lambda}^{2}(\mathbf q)
Q_{\lambda}^{\dagger}(\mathbf q)Q_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
\right],
\] where \(Q_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\) is the normal coordinate of a vibrational mode \(\lambda\) and \(P_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\) its conjugate momentum.
In this picture, a crystal is no longer described as \(3rN\) coupled atomic displacements, but as \(3rN\) independent harmonic modes labeled by \((\mathbf q,\lambda)\).
A useful sanity check is immediate:

one allowed \(\mathbf q\) value for each primitive cell,
\(3r\) branches for each \(\mathbf q\),
hence \(3rN\) modes in total.




8.2.2 Canonical Quantization of a Single Mode
For one harmonic mode of frequency \(\omega\), write the Hamiltonian \[
H=\frac{1}{2}\left(P^2+\omega^2Q^2\right)
\] and recall the corresponding commutation relation \([Q,P]=i\hbar\).
We now introduce the ladder operators \[
a=\sqrt{\frac{\omega}{2\hbar}}
\left(
Q+\frac{i}{\omega}P
\right),
\qquad
a^{\dagger}
=
\sqrt{\frac{\omega}{2\hbar}}
\left(
Q-\frac{i}{\omega}P
\right).
\]
Then \[
[a,a^{\dagger}]=1,
\] and the Hamiltonian becomes






\[
H=\hbar\omega\left(a^{\dagger}a+\frac{1}{2}\right).
\]



Therefore the energy eigenvalues are \[
E_n=\hbar\omega\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right),
\qquad
n=0,1,2,\dots
\]
The operator \(a^{\dagger}\) raises the excitation number by one quantum, while \(a\) lowers it by one.



8.2.3 Quantized Lattice Hamiltonian
Applying the quantized harmonic oscillator mode by mode gives






\[
H_{\mathrm{harm}}
=
\sum_{\mathbf q,\lambda}
\hbar\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
\left[
a_{\lambda}^{\dagger}(\mathbf q)a_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
+
\frac{1}{2}
\right].
\]



The number operator \[
\hat n_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
=
a_{\lambda}^{\dagger}(\mathbf q)a_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
\] has eigenvalues \(0,1,2,\dots\), so the crystal energy is built from discrete quanta \(\hbar\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\).
This is the origin of the phonon picture.



8.2.4 Phonons as Elementary Excitations
A phonon is the quantum of a lattice normal mode. For a mode \((\mathbf q,\lambda)\):

creating one phonon raises the energy by \(\hbar\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\),
annihilating one phonon lowers the energy by the same amount.

Because the operators satisfy bosonic commutation relations, phonons are bosons. Multiple phonons may occupy the same mode.
A further important point is that even the ground state is not completely motionless. Each mode contributes the zero-point energy \[
E_{0,\lambda}(\mathbf q)=\frac{1}{2}\hbar\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q).
\]
Thus the harmonic crystal retains quantum zero-point motion even at \(T=0\).

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.3 Thermal Population of Phonon Modes
text:
8.3 Thermal Population of Phonon Modes
Once the normal modes are quantized, thermal physics reduces to the thermal population of independent bosonic modes.


8.3.1 Partition Function of One Harmonic Mode
For one mode of frequency \(\omega\), the energy levels are \[
E_n=\hbar\omega\left(n+\frac{1}{2}\right).
\]
The canonical partition function is therefore \[
Z_{\omega}
=
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
e^{-\beta E_n}
=
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
e^{-\beta \hbar\omega(n+1/2)}
=
\frac{e^{-\beta\hbar\omega/2}}{1-e^{-\beta\hbar\omega}},
\] where \(\beta=\frac{1}{k_B T}\).
From this, we obtain the mean occupation number as \[
\langle n\rangle
= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n p_n
=\frac{1}{Z_{\omega}}
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
n e^{-\beta \hbar\omega(n+1/2)}
=\frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}.
\] Notice that phonons follow the Bose-Einstein statistics. Therefore it is convenient to name this quantity






\[
n_B(\omega,T)
=\frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}
=\frac{1}{e^{\hbar\omega/k_B T}-1}.
\]



Also note that for phonons, the chemical potential is zero because phonon number is not conserved.



8.3.2 Mean Energy of One Mode
The mean energy follows immediately: \[
\langle E_{\omega}\rangle
=
\hbar\omega\left(\langle n\rangle+\frac{1}{2}\right)
=
\hbar\omega
\left[
\frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}
+
\frac{1}{2}
\right].
\]
It is useful to split this into two pieces: \[
\langle E_{\omega}\rangle
=
\underbrace{
\frac{\hbar\omega}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}
}_{\text{thermal part}}
+
\underbrace{
\frac{1}{2}\hbar\omega
}_{\text{zero-point part}}.
\]
The thermal part vanishes as \(T\to 0\), while the zero-point part remains.



8.3.3 Two Limits Worth Remembering
High temperature
At high temperature, \(\beta\hbar\omega\ll 1\), so \[
n_B(\omega,T)\approx \frac{k_B T}{\hbar\omega},
\] and hence \[
\langle E_{\omega}\rangle
\approx
k_B T+\frac{1}{2}\hbar\omega.
\]
The temperature-dependent part becomes classical.
Low temperature
At low temperature, \(\beta\hbar\omega\gg 1\), so \[
n_B(\omega,T)\approx e^{-\beta\hbar\omega},
\] and thermal excitation is strongly suppressed.
This already hints at the key role of low-frequency modes: they are the easiest to populate thermally and dominate low-temperature thermodynamics.

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.4 Constructing the Phonon Density of States
text:
8.4 Constructing the Phonon Density of States
The phonon spectrum is usually too complicated to handle mode by mode. The density of states reorganizes the same information in the context of thermodynamics.


8.4.1 From Discrete \(\mathbf q\) Values to the Continuum
For a macroscopic crystal, allowed \(\mathbf q\) values are extremely dense. The sum over wavevectors may be replaced by an integral, \[
\sum_{\mathbf q}
\longrightarrow
\frac{V}{(2\pi)^3}\int d^3q.
\]
This replacement is the reciprocal-space version of passing from a finite crystal to the thermodynamic limit.
A good sanity check is the dimensionality: the factor \(V/(2\pi)^3\) has units of reciprocal-space density, exactly what is needed to count modes in a \(d^3q\) volume element.



8.4.2 Definition of the DOS
Define the partial DOS of branch \(\lambda\) and the total DOS by \[
g_{\lambda}(\omega)
=
\int \frac{d^3q}{(2\pi)^3}
\delta\left(\omega-\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\right),
\] \[
g(\omega)=\sum_{\lambda}g_{\lambda}(\omega).
\]
With this definition, for any function \(f(\omega)\), \[
\frac{1}{V}\sum_{\mathbf q,\lambda} f\left(\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\right)
=
\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega g(\omega) f(\omega).
\]
So \(g(\omega) d\omega\) is the number of phonon states per unit volume with frequencies between \(\omega\) and \(\omega+d\omega\).






\[
\frac{1}{V}\sum_{\mathbf q,\lambda} f\left(\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\right)
=
\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\ g(\omega) f(\omega).
\]






8.4.3 Geometric Interpretation
Let \(S_{\lambda}(\omega)\) be the constant-frequency surface in \(\mathbf q\)-space defined by \[
\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)=\omega.
\]
Then \[
g_{\lambda}(\omega)
=
\frac{1}{(2\pi)^3}
\int_{S_{\lambda}(\omega)}
\frac{dS}{\left|\nabla_{\mathbf q}\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)\right|}.
\]
This formula is physically very revealing:

where the dispersion is steep, \(\left|\nabla_{\mathbf q}\omega\right|\) is large and the DOS is small;
where the dispersion is flat, the DOS is enhanced;
if the group velocity vanishes on special parts of the spectrum, sharp DOS features can appear.

Thus the DOS is high where many nearby \(\mathbf q\) values correspond to nearly the same frequency.







Figure 8.1: Figure placeholder. Constant-frequency surfaces \(\omega\) and \(\omega+d\omega\) in reciprocal space. The DOS is obtained by counting the allowed \(\mathbf q\) values in the shell between them.





8.4.4 Debye Approximation
For low-frequency acoustic phonons, we can approximate the crystal as an isotropic elastic continuum. Then \[
\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)=c_{\lambda}|\mathbf q|,
\] with one longitudinal branch and two transverse branches.
For one branch, \[
g_{\lambda}(\omega)
=
\frac{1}{2\pi^2}\frac{\omega^2}{c_{\lambda}^3}.
\]
Summing the branches gives \[
g(\omega)
=
\frac{1}{2\pi^2}\omega^2
\left(
\frac{1}{c_L^3}+\frac{2}{c_T^3}
\right).
\]
If one introduces an effective Debye sound velocity \(c_D\) through \[
\frac{3}{c_D^3}
=
\frac{1}{c_L^3}+\frac{2}{c_T^3},
\] then \[
g(\omega)=\frac{3}{2\pi^2}\frac{\omega^2}{c_D^3}.
\]
This cannot hold to arbitrarily large frequency, so the Debye model imposes a cutoff \(\omega_D=c_D q_D\) chosen such that the total number of modes is still correct: \[
\frac{V}{(2\pi)^3}\frac{4\pi q_D^3}{3}=rN.
\]
Therefore the Debye DOS is






\[
g_D(\omega)
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{9rN}{V}\dfrac{\omega^2}{\omega_D^3}, & 0\le \omega\le \omega_D,\\
0, & \omega>\omega_D.
\end{cases}
\]



A crucial sanity check is normalization: \[
V\int_0^{\omega_D} g_D(\omega) d\omega
=
3rN.
\]
So the Debye model preserves the correct total number of vibrational modes even though it smooths out the detailed branch structure.



8.4.5 What the Debye Approximation Captures and What It Misses
The Debye DOS gets the low-frequency part right because in three dimensions:

acoustic branches are linear near \(\mathbf q=\mathbf 0\),
the area of a sphere in \(\mathbf q\)-space scales as \(q^2\),
and therefore \(g(\omega)\propto \omega^2\) for small \(\omega\).

However, the Debye DOS misses:

the detailed separation of acoustic and optical branches,
narrow peaks from weakly dispersive optical modes,
and sharper DOS features associated with Van Hove singularities.

Real phonon DOS curves therefore agree with the Debye form at low frequency, but deviate strongly at intermediate and high frequency.







Figure 8.2: Figure placeholder. Phonon DOS of silicon together with the smooth Debye approximation, showing good agreement only in the low-frequency regime.









Figure 8.3: Figure placeholder. Calculated phonon DOS of aluminum with characteristic Van Hove features arising from flat regions of the dispersion.





8.4.6 Reading a DOS Plot Physically
When you look at a phonon DOS curve, several quick interpretations are useful:

the total area counts all vibrational modes;
the low-frequency onset is controlled by acoustic branches;
broad smooth parts reflect rapidly varying dispersions;
sharp peaks indicate flat dispersions and slow group velocity;
nearly dispersionless optical modes often show up as narrow high-frequency structures.

This is why the DOS is such a useful quantity for representing the vibrational spectrum.

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.5 Bridge to the Next Lecture: Internal Energy as a DOS Integral
text:
8.5 Bridge to the Next Lecture: Internal Energy as a DOS Integral
In the next lecture, we will combine the quantized-mode picture and the DOS into a compact formula for the thermal energy of the lattice.
For the full harmonic crystal, \[
U(T)
=
\sum_{\mathbf q,\lambda}
\hbar\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q)
\left[
n_B\left(\omega_{\lambda}(\mathbf q),T\right)
+
\frac{1}{2}
\right].
\]
Using the DOS definition, this becomes






\[
U(T)
=
V\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\
g(\omega) \hbar\omega
\left[
n_B(\omega,T)+\frac{1}{2}
\right].
\]



Separating thermal and zero-point parts, \[
U(T)=U_{\mathrm{th}}(T)+U_0,
\] with \[
U_{\mathrm{th}}(T)
=
V\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\
g(\omega)
\frac{\hbar\omega}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1},
\] \[
U_0
=
\frac{V}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\ g(\omega) \hbar\omega.
\]
This is how heat capacity will come into play:

\(U_0\) is temperature independent in the harmonic approximation, so it does not contribute to \(C_V\);
the low-temperature behavior is controlled by the low-frequency DOS;
the high-temperature limit is controlled by DOS normalization.

In the next lecture, we will evaluate these integrals, especially with the Debye DOS, and extract the temperature dependence of the heat capacity.

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.6 Optional / Appendix: Einstein Model as a One-Frequency DOS
text:
8.6 Optional / Appendix: Einstein Model as a One-Frequency DOS


Open optional appendix

Another useful model for the DOS is the Einstein model, where all oscillators have the same frequency \(\omega_E\). This corresponds to \[
g_E(\omega)=\frac{3rN}{V} \delta(\omega-\omega_E).
\]
Then \[
U_{\mathrm{th}}(T)
=
3rN
\frac{\hbar\omega_E}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega_E}-1}.
\]
This captures quantization correctly but misses the acoustic low-frequency modes. That is why it gives a qualitatively wrong low-temperature heat capacity, whereas the Debye approximation succeeds there.








NoteTake-Home Messages




Quantizing lattice normal modes turns the harmonic crystal into a gas of independent bosonic excitations called phonons.
Each mode behaves like a quantum harmonic oscillator and retains zero-point motion even at zero temperature.
Thermal phonon populations follow Bose–Einstein statistics because many phonons can occupy the same mode.
Low-frequency modes are thermally the most important because they are easiest to excite.
The phonon DOS repackages the dispersion information into a form directly suited to thermodynamics.
In three dimensions, the low-frequency DOS grows quadratically with frequency for acoustic phonons.
The Debye model is a low-frequency approximation, not a detailed description of the full phonon spectrum.
Heat capacity will follow from differentiating the DOS integral for the internal energy.

title: 8  Phonons I
section: 8.7 Problem Set
text:
8.7 Problem Set

One mode, one oscillator. Starting from \[
H=\frac{1}{2}\left(P^2+\omega^2Q^2\right),
\qquad
[Q,P]=i\hbar,
\] define ladder operators and show that the Hamiltonian can be written as \[
H=\hbar\omega\left(a^{\dagger}a+\frac{1}{2}\right).
\] What are the energy eigenvalues?
Bose occupation of a phonon mode. For one phonon mode of frequency \(\omega\), compute the partition function \[
Z_{\omega}=\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} e^{-\beta \hbar\omega(n+1/2)}
\] and derive the mean occupation number \[
n_B(\omega,T)=\frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}.
\] Then obtain the mean energy of the mode.
Debye DOS and normalization. Assume an isotropic elastic continuum with one longitudinal sound velocity \(c_L\) and two transverse sound velocities \(c_T\). Derive \[
g(\omega)
=
\frac{1}{2\pi^2}\omega^2
\left(
\frac{1}{c_L^3}+\frac{2}{c_T^3}
\right).
\] Then show that the Debye cutoff can be chosen so that the total number of modes is \(3rN\).
High-temperature limit from the DOS. Starting from \[
U_{\mathrm{th}}(T)
=
V\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega
g(\omega)
\frac{\hbar\omega}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1},
\] show that if \(k_B T\gg \hbar\omega\) over the relevant spectrum and the DOS is normalized to \(3rN\) modes, then \[
U_{\mathrm{th}}(T)\approx 3rN k_B T.
\] What follows for the heat capacity?

title: 9  Phonons II
section: 9.2 From the Phonon DOS to Heat Capacity
text:
9.2 From the Phonon DOS to Heat Capacity
We take the end point of Lecture 8 as the starting point here: once the phonon density of states is known, lattice thermodynamics reduces to a one-dimensional frequency integral:






\[
U(T)
=
V\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\
g(\omega)\hbar\omega
\left[
\frac{1}{e^{\beta\hbar\omega}-1}
+
\frac{1}{2}
\right].
\]



Here \(U(T)\) is the lattice internal energy, \(g(\omega)\) is the phonon density of states per unit volume, and \(\beta=1/(k_B T)\) is the inverse temperature. The term \(\hbar\omega/2\) is the zero-point contribution of each mode.

The heat capacity is defined as the derivative of the internal energy of the lattice with respect to temperature \[
C_V = \frac{\partial U(T)}{\partial T}
\]
This gives






\[
C_V
=
V k_B
\int_{0}^{\infty} d\omega\
g(\omega)
\frac{x^2 e^x}{(e^x-1)^2},
\qquad
x=\frac{\hbar\omega}{k_B T}.
\]



Since the zero-point term is temperature independent in the harmonic approximation, it drops out of the heat capacity.
The dimensionless variable \(x\) compares the phonon energy \(\hbar\omega\) with the thermal scale \(k_B T\). This formula shows immediately that the heat capacity is controlled by two inputs only: the Bose factor and the phonon DOS.

A useful sanity check:

low-frequency modes have small \(x\) and are easy to excite;
high-frequency modes have large \(x\) and are thermally frozen out;
therefore the low-\(\omega\) part of \(g(\omega)\) controls low-temperature thermodynamics.

title: 9  Phonons II
section: 9.3 Einstein Model: Quantum Oscillators at One Frequency
text:
9.3 Einstein Model: Quantum Oscillators at One Frequency
The Einstein model is the simplest quantum model of lattice heat capacity: all vibrational modes are assigned the same frequency \(\omega_E\). It is therefore a model with the correct quantum statistics but an oversimplified spectrum.

9.3.1 Einstein DOS and Heat Capacity
In DOS language, the Einstein model is
\[
g_E(\omega)
=
\frac{3rN}{V}\ \delta(\omega-\omega_E).
\]
The prefactor counts the \(3rN\) vibrational modes of the crystal, and the delta function means that all of them sit at the same angular frequency \(\omega_E\).

Substituting \(g_E(\omega)\) into the general formula for \(C_V\) gives






\[
C_V^{(E)}
=
3rN k_B
\frac{x_E^2 e^{x_E}}{(e^{x_E}-1)^2},
\qquad
x_E=\frac{\hbar\omega_E}{k_B T}=\frac{\Theta_E}{T}.
\]



Here \(\Theta_E\) is the Einstein temperature, defined by \(k_B\Theta_E=\hbar\omega_E\). The factor \[
F_E(x)=\frac{x^2 e^x}{(e^x-1)^2}
\] is the Einstein function.

Interpretation:

one model parameter, \(\omega_E\) or equivalently \(\Theta_E\);
all branches are treated as if they were equally hard to excite;
no true acoustic low-energy continuum is present.



9.3.2 High-Temperature Limit: Dulong–Petit Recovered
For \(T \gg \Theta_E\), one has \(x_E \ll 1\), and therefore
\[
\frac{x_E^2 e^{x_E}}{(e^{x_E}-1)^2}
=
1+\mathcal O(x_E^2).
\]
Hence






\[
C_V^{(E)}
\longrightarrow
3rN k_B.
\]



This is the Dulong–Petit law. Per mole of primitive cells, the corresponding limit is \(3rR\). In this regime every vibrational mode contributes essentially \(k_B\) to the heat capacity.

The high-temperature result is therefore not a special property of the Einstein model. It follows much more generally from mode counting plus classical equipartition.


9.3.3 Why the Einstein Model Fails at Low Temperature
For \(T \ll \Theta_E\), one has \(x_E \gg 1\), so
\[
\frac{x_E^2 e^{x_E}}{(e^{x_E}-1)^2}
\sim
x_E^2 e^{-x_E}.
\]
Therefore






\[
C_V^{(E)}
\sim
3rN k_B
\left(\frac{\Theta_E}{T}\right)^2
e^{-\Theta_E/T}.
\]



The heat capacity falls off exponentially.

This is too fast. The physical reason is simple: the Einstein model has no modes with arbitrarily small \(\omega\). Real crystals do. Long-wavelength acoustic phonons cost very little energy and remain thermally accessible even when \(T\) is small. The Einstein model therefore captures quantization, but it suppresses the very modes that dominate low-temperature thermodynamics.







Figure 9.1: Figure placeholder. Specific heat in the Einstein model as a function of reduced temperature, compared with experimental data for diamond. The model improves substantially over the classical result but still decays too rapidly at low temperature.

title: 9  Phonons II
section: 9.4 Debye Model: Acoustic Modes Dominate Low-Temperature Thermodynamics
text:
9.4 Debye Model: Acoustic Modes Dominate Low-Temperature Thermodynamics
The Debye model keeps the one ingredient the Einstein model misses: an acoustic continuum with linear dispersion at low frequency.

9.4.1 Debye DOS and the Debye Temperature
In Lecture 8 we obtained the Debye density of states
\[
g_D(\omega)
=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{9rN}{V}\dfrac{\omega^2}{\omega_D^3}, & 0\le \omega \le \omega_D,\\
0, & \omega>\omega_D.
\end{cases}
\]
The cutoff \(\omega_D\) is chosen so that the total number of modes remains correct: \[
V\int_{0}^{\omega_D}\ g_D(\omega) d\omega = 3rN.
\]
Thus the Debye model does not preserve the detailed branch structure of the real phonon spectrum, but it does preserve the total number of modes and the correct low-frequency behavior \(g(\omega)\propto \omega^2\).

The Debye temperature is defined by \[
k_B\Theta_D=\hbar\omega_D.
\]
It packages the cutoff energy into a temperature scale. In the Debye model, once \(\Theta_D\) is fixed, the entire temperature dependence of the lattice heat capacity is fixed as well.



9.4.2 Debye Internal Energy and Heat Capacity
We insert \(g_D(\omega)\) into the thermal part of the internal energy, omit the zero-point term, and change variables to \[
t=\frac{\hbar\omega}{k_B T}.
\] The symbol \(t\) is dimensionless; it measures phonon frequency in units of temperature.
We obtain
\[
U_T
=
9rN k_B T
\left(\frac{T}{\Theta_D}\right)^3
\int_{0}^{\Theta_D/T} dt\
\frac{t^3}{e^t-1} .
\]
Differentiating with respect to \(T\) yields the Debye heat capacity






\[
C_V^{(D)}
=
9rN k_B
\left(\frac{T}{\Theta_D}\right)^3
\int_{0}^{\Theta_D/T} dt\
\frac{t^4 e^t}{(e^t-1)^2} .
\]



This is the central working formula of the Debye model.

Sanity checks:

the prefactor is extensive in the number of modes, \(3rN\);
the only material parameter is \(\Theta_D\);
the entire nontrivial temperature dependence sits in a dimensionless integral.







Figure 9.2: Figure placeholder. Debye-model specific heat as a universal function of reduced temperature \(T/\Theta_D\), normalized by the Dulong–Petit value.





9.4.3 Low-Temperature Limit: The \(T^{3}\) Law
For \(T \ll \Theta_D\), the upper integration limit \(\Theta_D/T\) is very large, so the integral may be extended to infinity:
\[
\int_{0}^{\Theta_D/T} dt\
\frac{t^4 e^t}{(e^t-1)^2}
\approx
\int_{0}^{\infty} dt\
\frac{t^4 e^t}{(e^t-1)^2}
=
\frac{4\pi^4}{15}.
\]
Therefore






\[
C_V^{(D)}
\approx
\frac{12\pi^4}{5} r N k_B
\left(\frac{T}{\Theta_D}\right)^3
\qquad
T\ll \Theta_D.
\]



This is the Debye \(T^3\) law.

Why \(T^3\)?

in three dimensions, the acoustic DOS starts as \(g(\omega)\propto\omega^2\);
the relevant thermally occupied frequencies satisfy \(\hbar\omega \lesssim k_B T\);
counting modes up to \(\omega\sim T\) therefore gives a phase-space factor \(\sim T^3\).

So the low-temperature heat capacity is not an arbitrary curve fit. It is a direct fingerprint of three-dimensional acoustic phonons.



9.4.4 High-Temperature Limit: Dulong–Petit Recovered Again
For \(T \gg \Theta_D\), the upper limit \(\Theta_D/T\) is small, and the Bose factor may be expanded as \[
e^t-1 \approx t.
\]
Equivalently, each mode approaches the classical result \(\langle E\rangle \approx k_B T\). Since the total number of modes is \(3rN\), one recovers






\[
C_V^{(D)}
\longrightarrow
3rN k_B,
\qquad
T\gg \Theta_D.
\]



So both Einstein and Debye recover Dulong–Petit at high temperature, but only Debye gives the correct low-temperature asymptote.



9.4.5 What the Debye Temperature Means
We can interpret the Debye temperature \(\Theta_D\) in the following way:

\(\Theta_D\) is the temperature scale associated with the Debye cutoff energy \(\hbar\omega_D\);
it increases with increasing characteristic sound velocity and with stiffer bonding;
it marks the crossover between the low-temperature acoustic regime and the high-temperature Dulong–Petit regime;
it is model dependent.

The last point matters in practice. Real solids do not have an exact Debye DOS over the whole spectrum, so a \(\Theta_D\) extracted from the low-temperature \(T^3\) coefficient need not coincide with a value inferred from fitting the full heat-capacity curve at higher temperature.

title: 9  Phonons II
section: 9.5 Data Literacy: How to Read Low-Temperature Heat-Capacity Data
text:
9.5 Data Literacy: How to Read Low-Temperature Heat-Capacity Data
The Debye law becomes especially useful when it is converted into a fitting form.

9.5.1 Insulators: Extracting the Phonon Coefficient
For a nonmetallic crystal at sufficiently low temperature, the leading lattice contribution is
\[
C_{V,m}
\approx
\beta_{\mathrm{LT}} T^3.
\]
Here \(C_{V,m}\) is the molar heat capacity, and \(\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}\) is the fitted low-temperature phonon coefficient. For one mole of the chosen chemical unit, the coefficient is
\[
\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}
=
\frac{12\pi^4}{5}
\frac{n_{\mathrm{at}} R}{\Theta_D^3}.
\]
The integer \(n_{\mathrm{at}}\) is the number of atoms in the chemical unit used to report the molar heat capacity. For a monatomic elemental solid quoted per mole of atoms, \(n_{\mathrm{at}}=1\).

Thus \[
\Theta_D
=
\left(
\frac{12\pi^4 n_{\mathrm{at}} R}{5\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}}
\right)^{1/3}.
\]
This is the standard route from a low-temperature fit to an effective Debye temperature.


9.5.2 Metals: Why Plot \(C/T\) Versus \(T^2\)?
In a metal, the low-temperature heat capacity contains both lattice and electronic pieces. Keeping only the leading terms,






\[
C_{V,m}
\approx
\gamma T+\beta_{\mathrm{LT}} T^3.
\]



Dividing by \(T\) gives






\[
\frac{C_{V,m}}{T}
\approx
\gamma+\beta_{\mathrm{LT}} T^2.
\]



Now the logic is purely graphical:

plot \(C_{V,m}/T\) on the vertical axis;
plot \(T^2\) on the horizontal axis;
the intercept is \(\gamma\);
the slope is \(\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}\).

This is why \(C/T\) versus \(T^2\) is such a standard low-temperature analysis plot.

The linear term \(\gamma T\) is absent in ionic insulators but present in metals. It comes from the conduction electrons, not from the phonons. We only mention it here and will derive it in the upcoming lecture when we introduce the electron gas.







Figure 9.3: Figure placeholder. Low-temperature heat-capacity data plotted as \(C_V/T\) versus \(T^2\) for an ionic crystal and a metal. The insulating sample is nearly a straight line through the origin, whereas the metal shows a finite intercept from the electronic term.





9.5.3 What a Straight Line Does and Does Not Mean
A straight line in \(C/T\) versus \(T^2\) means:

the data are in a regime where the leading terms \(\gamma T\) and \(\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}T^3\) dominate;
the phonon part is consistent with Debye’s low-temperature asymptote;
one can extract \(\gamma\) and \(\beta_{\mathrm{LT}}\) with minimal modeling.

It does not mean:

the Debye model is exact at all temperatures;
the full phonon DOS is featureless;
the same \(\Theta_D\) must fit the entire heat-capacity curve.

That last point is important whenever one compares low-temperature fits with room-temperature material tables.



9.5.4 Experimental Note
Strictly speaking, the derivations above concern \(C_V\). In many low-temperature solid-state measurements, the experimentally accessible quantity is closer to \(C_P\), but at low temperature the difference is usually very small, so the same fitting logic is routinely applied. Within the strictly harmonic approximation, the two are equal.

title: 9  Phonons II
section: 9.6 Bridge to the Next Lecture: Why the Pure Phonon Picture Is Not the Whole Story
text:
9.6 Bridge to the Next Lecture: Why the Pure Phonon Picture Is Not the Whole Story
Today’s main message is that the harmonic phonon gas already explains two cornerstone facts:

the Dulong–Petit limit at high temperature;
the \(T^3\) lattice law at low temperature.

But real solids often show additional structure:

metals carry a linear electronic term \(\gamma T\) at low temperature;
anharmonicity becomes important at higher temperature;
then thermal expansion appears, phonons acquire finite lifetimes, and eventually \(C_P\) and \(C_V\) are no longer effectively identical.

So the next conceptual step is to consider the contribution from electrons and anharmonic effects.







NoteTake-Home Messages




The heat capacity of a harmonic crystal is determined by the phonon DOS together with Bose statistics.
The Einstein model fixes the classical overestimate of specific heat but misses the low-frequency acoustic modes.
The Debye model succeeds at low temperature because it builds in an acoustic DOS that starts quadratically in frequency.
The low-temperature \(T^3\) law is a phase-space result for three-dimensional acoustic phonons.
The Dulong–Petit limit is recovered when all vibrational modes are thermally populated.
The Debye temperature is a crossover scale and an effective stiffness measure, not a phase-transition temperature.
In low-temperature experiments, the slope of \(C/T\) versus \(T^2\) isolates the phonon coefficient, while the intercept isolates the electronic one.
A single fitted Debye temperature should always be interpreted together with the temperature range from which it was extracted.

\] derive the high-temperature and low-temperature asymptotic forms. State clearly which physical feature of the model causes the low-temperature behavior.

\] show that for \(T\ll\Theta_D\), \[

=
